title: NP-hard problems naturally arising in knot theory
speaker: Anastasiia Tsvietkova
abstract: Low-dimensional topology and geometry have many problems with an easy formulation, but a hard solution. Despite our intuitive feeling that these problems are "hard", lower or upper bounds on algorithmic complexity are known only for some of them. Recently, several problems that lie at the heart of classical knot theory were shown to be NP-hard. We will discuss the problems related to unlinking and splitting by crossing changes, to Reidemeister moves, and to detecting sublinks with various properties. Based on joint work with Dale Koenig.

proof_stages.label: Decision-problem and NP-hardness framework
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recasts low-dimensional topology questions as decision problems and fixes the complexity language of NP certificates, SAT, Karp reductions, and the conditional meaning of NP-hard lower bounds.

proof_stages.label: Known lower bounds and natural gaps
proof_stages.summary: A survey of previously known hardness results for genus-type and sublink questions, together with the limited lower bounds for 3-manifold homeomorphism and link crossing number, motivates seeking NP-hardness for more central knot-theoretic problems.

proof_stages.label: Target knot decision problems
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates Reidemeister-move distance, unknotting/unlinking/splitting numbers, and alternating-sublink detection, stating which of these are proved NP-hard and which remain open.

proof_stages.label: Brunnian clause gadget for unlink-as-sublink
proof_stages.summary: A 3-SAT instance is encoded using Hopf-link variable pairs and Brunnian commutator clause components so that satisfiable truth assignments are exactly the choices producing an unlink sublink with the prescribed number of components.

proof_stages.label: Whitehead-doubled reduction toward unlinking number
proof_stages.summary: The unlink-as-sublink construction is modified by replacing each Hopf variable gadget with a Whitehead-doubled Hopf link, so that choosing a truth value corresponds to a prescribed one-crossing unclasping in the displayed diagram.

proof_stages.label: Diagram-independent unlinking-move normalization
proof_stages.summary: Unlinking moves are introduced as diagram-independent versions of crossing changes, and a separation lemma shows that arbitrary unlinking sequences can be replaced by the prescribed unclaspings without increasing their length.

proof_stages.label: Splitting-number adaptation
proof_stages.summary: The unlinking-number reduction is adapted using separated Whitehead doubles and an extra cabled component to force the same number of crossing changes, yielding NP-hardness of the splitting-number decision problem.
